reason: A low buffer pool hit rate indicates InnoDB is reading more data from disk instead of memory. The manual states that a larger buffer pool lets InnoDB behave more like an in-memory database and speeds subsequent reads.
recommend: Increase innodb_buffer_pool_size as large as practical while leaving enough memory for other processes to avoid excessive paging.

source.title: Buffer Pool Configuration
source.quote: Ideally, you set the size of the buffer pool to as large a value as practical, leaving enough memory for other processes on the server to run without excessive paging.

reason: Pages being evicted without ever being accessed suggests scans or read-ahead are polluting the buffer pool and displacing useful cached pages. The document warns that large table scans can evict older data even if the new data is never reused.
recommend: Make the buffer pool more scan resistant by tuning old-sublist behavior, especially innodb_old_blocks_time and possibly innodb_old_blocks_pct.
fix: SET PERSIST innodb_old_blocks_time = <milliseconds>; SET PERSIST innodb_old_blocks_pct = <percent>;

source.title: Buffer Pool LRU Algorithm
source.quote: A table scan, performed for a mysqldump operation or a SELECT statement with no WHERE clause, for example, can bring a large amount of data into the buffer pool and evict an equivalent amount of older data, even if the new data is never used again.

reason: Very low youngs/s without large scans means old pages are not being promoted effectively, reducing the chance that useful pages stay cached. The manual explicitly recommends adjusting delay time or old-sublist percentage in this case.
recommend: Reduce innodb_old_blocks_time or increase innodb_old_blocks_pct to improve promotion of useful old pages.
fix: SET PERSIST innodb_old_blocks_time = <milliseconds>; SET PERSIST innodb_old_blocks_pct = <percent>;

source.title: Monitoring the Buffer Pool Using the InnoDB Standard Monitor
source.quote: If you see very low youngs/s values when there are no large scans occurring, consider reducing the delay time or increasing the percentage of the buffer pool used for the old sublist.

reason: If non-youngs/s does not rise during large table scans, scan pages may be promoted too aggressively and pollute the cache. The manual recommends increasing the delay value to make the buffer pool more scan resistant.
recommend: Increase innodb_old_blocks_time so pages brought in by scans are less likely to be made young immediately.

source.title: Monitoring the Buffer Pool Using the InnoDB Standard Monitor
source.quote: If you do not see a higher non-youngs/s value when performing large table scans (and a higher youngs/s value), increase the delay value.

reason: A single large buffer pool can increase contention on memory structures under concurrent workloads. The manual recommends splitting the buffer pool into multiple parts on 64-bit systems with sufficient memory.
recommend: Increase innodb_buffer_pool_instances when running a large buffer pool on a 64-bit server with concurrent operations.

source.title: Buffer Pool Configuration
source.quote: On 64-bit systems with sufficient memory, you can split the buffer pool into multiple parts to minimize contention for memory structures among concurrent operations.

reason: Disabling the doublewrite buffer removes torn-page protection during crashes and also forgoes a mechanism the manual says improves performance on most Unix systems by reducing fsync() operations.
recommend: Enable InnoDB doublewrite unless you have a very specific, validated storage-stack reason to disable it.

source.title: Doublewrite Buffer
source.quote: InnoDB uses a novel file flush technique involving a structure called the doublewrite buffer, which is enabled by default in most cases (innodb_doublewrite=ON). It adds safety to recovery following an unexpected exit or power outage, and improves performance on most varieties of Unix by reducing the need for fsync() operations.

reason: If a large share of physical reads comes from read-ahead activity, prefetching may be pulling in pages that are not actually needed soon, reducing buffer-pool efficiency. The manual describes read-ahead as beneficial only when InnoDB can determine there is a high probability the data will be needed soon.
recommend: Review and tune InnoDB read-ahead heuristics for the workload, especially if access is not strongly sequential or full-area scans are uncommon.

source.title: Read-Ahead
source.quote: If InnoDB can determine there is a high probability that data might be needed soon, it performs read-ahead operations to bring that data into the buffer pool so that it is available in memory.

reason: The manual notes that a few large contiguous reads are more efficient than many small scattered reads. Elevated physical reads per statement can indicate query access patterns that defeat sequential I/O and read-ahead benefits.
recommend: Rewrite queries and add or adjust indexes so access becomes more contiguous and selective, reducing scattered page reads.

source.title: Read-Ahead
source.quote: Making a few large read requests for contiguous data can be more efficient than making several small, spread-out requests.

reason: A high share of full table scans on SELECTs suggests predicates are not supported by indexes, causing slower row filtering and retrieval.
recommend: Create indexes on columns commonly tested in WHERE clauses for frequently executed SELECT statements.

source.title: 10.3 Optimization and Indexes
source.quote: The best way to improve the performance of SELECT operations is to create indexes on one or more of the columns that are tested in the query.

reason: Heavy write workloads suffer when too many indexes exist because each INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE must maintain every index.
recommend: Remove unnecessary indexes and keep only the optimal set that materially improves important queries.

source.title: 10.3 Optimization and Indexes
source.quote: Indexes also add to the cost of inserts, updates, and deletes because each index must be updated.

reason: Excess indexes consume storage and increase optimizer work when choosing access paths, which can reduce overall efficiency.
recommend: Audit index usage and drop redundant or low-value indexes to keep a balanced, optimal set.

source.title: 10.3 Optimization and Indexes
source.quote: Although it can be tempting to create an indexes for every possible column used in a query, unnecessary indexes waste space and waste time for MySQL to determine which indexes to use.

reason: Under REPEATABLE READ, all consistent reads in a transaction use the snapshot established by the first read, so long-running transactions can keep reading stale data and delay seeing newly committed changes.
recommend: Keep transactions short; commit before issuing new reads when you need a fresher snapshot.

source.title: 17.7.2.3 Consistent Nonlocking Reads
source.quote: If the transaction isolation level is REPEATABLE READ (the default level), all consistent reads within the same transaction read the snapshot established by the first such read in that transaction. You can get a fresher snapshot for your queries by committing the current transaction and after that issuing new queries.

reason: REPEATABLE READ preserves a transaction snapshot across reads, which can be undesirable for workloads that need the freshest committed view on each SELECT.
recommend: Use READ COMMITTED for sessions or workloads that require fresher snapshots on each consistent read.
fix: SET SESSION TRANSACTION ISOLATION LEVEL READ COMMITTED;

source.title: 17.7.2.3 Consistent Nonlocking Reads
source.quote: With READ COMMITTED isolation level, each consistent read within a transaction sets and reads its own fresh snapshot.

reason: Ordinary consistent reads are nonlocking and may not see the newest committed rows under REPEATABLE READ; a locking read can force waiting for the freshest rows to become visible.
recommend: For queries that must see the freshest state, use a locking read such as FOR SHARE instead of a plain SELECT.

source.title: 17.7.2.3 Consistent Nonlocking Reads
source.quote: If you want to see the “freshest” state of the database, use either the READ COMMITTED isolation level or a locking read: SELECT * FROM t FOR SHARE;

reason: Consistent reads do not work across certain DDL operations such as DROP TABLE and some ALTER TABLE forms, causing transaction retries and runtime errors.
recommend: Avoid running conflicting DDL concurrently with long transactions that rely on consistent reads; retry the transaction after the DDL completes.
fix: COMMIT; START TRANSACTION WITH CONSISTENT SNAPSHOT;

source.title: 17.7.2.3 Consistent Nonlocking Reads
source.quote: Consistent read does not work over ALTER TABLE operations that make a temporary copy of the original table and delete the original table when the temporary copy is built... the transaction returns an error: ER_TABLE_DEF_CHANGED, “Table definition has changed, please retry transaction”.

reason: If the Performance Schema is disabled, low-level runtime execution data is unavailable, reducing visibility into waits, I/O, locks, and statement timing needed for performance tuning.
recommend: Enable the Performance Schema so runtime performance data is collected for analysis.

source.title: Chapter 29 MySQL Performance Schema
source.quote: The MySQL Performance Schema is a feature for monitoring MySQL Server execution at a low level.

metric_expr: SUM(CASE WHEN NAME IN ('events_statements_history','events_statements_history_long','events_waits_history','events_waits_history_long') AND ENABLED='YES' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) FROM performance_schema.setup_consumers
reason: Without current/history consumers enabled, you lose event histories and summaries that help determine how often activities occur and how much time they take.
recommend: Enable appropriate Performance Schema consumers for statement and wait history collection.
fix: UPDATE performance_schema.setup_consumers SET ENABLED = 'YES' WHERE NAME IN ('events_statements_history','events_statements_history_long','events_waits_history','events_waits_history_long');

source.title: Chapter 29 MySQL Performance Schema
source.quote: Current events are available, as well as event histories and summaries. This enables you to determine how many times instrumented activities were performed and how much time they took.

metric_expr: SUM(CASE WHEN ENABLED='YES' AND TIMED='YES' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) FROM performance_schema.setup_instruments
reason: If no instruments are enabled and timed, the server cannot collect timing information for instrumented events, limiting root-cause analysis of performance issues.
recommend: Enable relevant Performance Schema instruments and timing for the subsystems you need to analyze.
fix: UPDATE performance_schema.setup_instruments SET ENABLED = 'YES', TIMED = 'YES' WHERE NAME LIKE 'wait/%' OR NAME LIKE 'statement/%';

source.title: Chapter 29 MySQL Performance Schema
source.quote: An “event” is anything the server does that takes time and has been instrumented so that timing information can be collected.

reason: Inefficient queries against Performance Schema can add avoidable overhead during analysis, especially if they rely on full scans instead of indexed access.
recommend: Rewrite Performance Schema queries to use indexed columns and prefer sys schema views where appropriate.
fix: EXPLAIN SELECT * FROM performance_schema.events_statements_summary_by_digest WHERE DIGEST IS NOT NULL;

source.title: Chapter 29 MySQL Performance Schema
source.quote: Most Performance Schema tables have indexes, which gives the optimizer access to execution plans other than full table scans.

reason: If the slow query log is disabled, the server does not capture slow statements, making it harder to identify queries that are candidates for optimization.
recommend: Enable the slow query log so slow statements are recorded for analysis.

source.title: Slow Query Log Parameters
source.quote: By default, the slow query log is disabled.

reason: Enabling the slow query log alone is insufficient if log_output is NONE, because no slow queries are actually written anywhere.
recommend: Set log_output to FILE or TABLE so enabled slow logging produces usable records.

source.title: Slow Query Log Contents
source.quote: If the destination is NONE, the server writes no queries even if the slow query log is enabled.

reason: A high long_query_time captures only very slow statements and can miss moderately slow queries that are still worth optimizing. The manual notes the default is 10 seconds.
recommend: Lower long_query_time to a more sensitive threshold appropriate for your workload to capture optimization candidates earlier.

source.title: Slow Query Log Parameters
source.quote: The minimum and default values of long_query_time are 0 and 10, respectively.

reason: Logging all non-indexed queries without throttling can cause the slow query log to grow rapidly, increasing overhead and making analysis harder.
recommend: Apply a per-minute throttle when logging queries that do not use indexes.

source.title: Slow Query Log Parameters
source.quote: When queries that do not use an index are logged, the slow query log may grow quickly. It is possible to put a rate limit on these queries by setting the log_throttle_queries_not_using_indexes system variable.
